name: Signature Intent
summary: Declarative capture of a deliberate signature commitment: a signer affirms their identity and intent, producing an immutable Signature Record (signer, timestamp, method). Keyboard-first — a typed legal name is the accessible primary path; a drawn mark is an enhancement, never the only way to sign. Captures the act of affirming a document; cryptographic sealing and legal-compliance audit are deliberately out of scope (app/adapter concerns layered on top). Distinct from web-identity (who you are / authentication) and from commitment-policy (when a form commits validation).

method.description: How the signature is captured. typed = a legal name via keyboard (the accessible primary path); drawn = a freehand canvas mark (an enhancement, with the typed path still present).

affirmation.description: The explicit intent-to-sign the signer must accept before the signature commits. required = a signature cannot be emitted until affirmed (the default); none = capture without an affirmation gate (rare; e.g. an internal initialing flow).

description:
Why a standard

Electronic signatures recur across origination, contracts, consent, and onboarding flows, and are almost always hand-rolled as a bespoke text input plus a timestamp. The Signature Intent captures the shared UX particularities — a deliberate affirmation of intent, a recorded signer identity and timestamp, and an immutable record — without prescribing presentation.

Keyboard-first capture

A typed legal name is the primary, accessible path and is always available. A drawn mark (canvas) is an enhancement — offered on top of, never instead of, typing — mirroring the data-transfer file-picker-primary principle. Affirmation is a structural precondition: no Signature Record is emitted until the signer accepts the author-supplied affirmation statement.

Scope boundary

  • In scope: the capture surface, the affirmation gate, and the immutable record (signer, timestamp, method, value, affirmed).
  • Out of scope: cryptographic signing / PKI (a sealing adapter), and legal-compliance audit (ESIGN/UETA) — composed app-side with the audit-trail block, not baked into the record.
  • Not this: web-identity establishes who you are (authentication); a signature is the act of affirming a document by an already-identified actor.

Interface

type SignatureMethod = 'typed' | 'drawn';

interface SignatureRecord {
  signer: string;        // the affirmed identity (typed legal name or supplied signer)
  signedAt: string;      // ISO-8601 timestamp
  method: SignatureMethod;
  value: string;         // the typed name, or a data-URL for a drawn mark
  affirmed: boolean;     // the intent-to-sign the signer accepted
}

interface SignatureIntent {
  method: SignatureMethod;       // typed (primary) | drawn (enhancement)
  affirmation: 'required' | 'none';
}
